video_title: MacroVoices #537 Brent Johnson: There's No Turning Back
gist: Santiago Capital's Brent Johnson argues the US-Iran MOU is a fragile pause rather than a real peace, that the Strait of Hormuz closure has set up delayed agricultural and energy shocks in Q4 2026/Q1 2027, and that dollar de-dollarization is a structural myth — with dollar-denominated stablecoins now becoming a new, underappreciated arm of US monetary dominance.

themes.theme: Iran MOU: Tenuous Pause, Not Resolution

insights.claim: The US-Iran MOU is best described as a managed pause rather than a genuine resolution: neither the US, Iran, nor Israel is likely to fully honor their obligations, and Brent Johnson explicitly expects a re-escalation within weeks to a few months.
insights.speaker: Brent Johnson
insights.so_what: Investors pricing the Iran conflict as 'over' are likely wrong; oil-market and geopolitical risk premiums that unwound sharply may need to be rebuilt.
insights.quote: I think the kinetic conflict is over for now and we're winding down for now, but I do not expect it to last.

insights.claim: The MOU contains deliberately ambiguous language: each side is interpreting its obligations differently, particularly on weapons-grade uranium removal (Iran/US say 'down-blend on site'; Israel insists on physical removal) and Lebanon ceasefire terms that Israel explicitly rejects.
insights.speaker: Erik Townsend
insights.so_what: The deal has structural fault lines that could cause it to collapse quickly; watch Israel's actions in Lebanon as the tripwire.

insights.claim: The US version of the MOU acknowledges a $300 billion (not $300 million) payment framework for Iran to be funded by third parties not party to the agreement — a mechanism Townsend considers implausible and likely to unravel.
insights.speaker: Erik Townsend
insights.so_what: The financial architecture of the deal is a potential deal-breaker; if the $300B mechanism fails, expect re-escalation and oil price spike.

insights.claim: Iran's parliament speaker announced immediately after the episode was recorded that the Strait of Hormuz will never return to its pre-war configuration and that Iran retains the right to charge transit fees — consistent with Johnson's prediction that a re-escalation or permanent Iranian toll is the likely outcome.
insights.speaker: Erik Townsend
insights.so_what: If Iran permanently controls Hormuz and charges fees, global tanker economics and energy pricing structures change fundamentally; long Iran toll-beneficiary scenarios.

insights.claim: Both the US and Iranian versions of the MOU explicitly state that the Strait of Hormuz will be toll-free for only 60 days, after which Iran charges a $1/barrel user fee (approximately $2 million per VLCC), meaning both sides effectively codified permanent Iranian toll authority over the strait.
insights.speaker: Erik Townsend
insights.so_what: A $1/barrel permanent levy on roughly 20% of global oil trade is a lasting structural cost; could be bullish for non-Hormuz routing infrastructure (pipelines, alternate transit routes).
insights.quote: Both versions of theou say that's what it goes back to after 60 days... Iran has basically been authorized to construct a permanent lifetime toll booth over the Straight of Hormuz.

insights.claim: Johnson believes the US pushed for a deal now — despite incomplete terms — because global petroleum reserves were being drawn down to dangerous levels, commercial US inventories hit operational minimums, and the US Strategic Petroleum Reserve fell to its lowest level since 1983, meaning inaction risked a catastrophic supply crisis.
insights.speaker: Brent Johnson
insights.so_what: The US negotiated from a position of energy weakness; the SPR needs to be rebuilt — a structural buyer of physical crude regardless of the diplomatic outcome.

themes.theme: Oil Price Outlook: Forced Selloff Sets Up Reversal

insights.claim: The ~20% oil price collapse from ~$90 to ~$73 on the Iran peace announcement was driven by forced liquidation (margin calls, CTA flips, stop losses) rather than a real assessment of supply recovery; both Townsend and Anas Al-Hajj believe oil has already overshot to the downside.
insights.speaker: Erik Townsend
insights.so_what: Once forced selling exhausts, oil likely rebounds; large gap on the chart at ~$84.50 is a near-term technical target.
insights.quote: There is a a huge structural repositioning in oil that has driven these flows to the downside. Obviously once those forced flows are unwound there is going to be a reaction higher.

insights.claim: Townsend revised his oil target: he no longer expects $150-$200/barrel by year-end because China can buffer the crisis by releasing its own SPR (which is substantially larger than the US SPR) and resuming finished-product exports, but he still targets a rebound to the $85-$100 range.
insights.speaker: Erik Townsend
insights.so_what: The ceiling on the bullish oil trade is lower than feared; China's SPR is an effective global shock absorber, limiting extreme price spikes.
insights.quote: I don't think we're headed to 200 anymore, but I think getting back to the 85 to 100 level is quite likely.

insights.claim: Even if the Strait reopened immediately, it would take at minimum 30 days to normalize traffic flow, then an additional 6 weeks for ships departing today to reach their destinations — meaning physical supply relief is at least 10 weeks out from the episode date.
insights.speaker: Erik Townsend
insights.so_what: Markets celebrating an immediate supply resolution are structurally wrong about the timeline; physical tightness will persist into late summer/early fall regardless of the diplomatic outcome.

insights.claim: Dr. Anas Al-Hajj, a well-known oil bear, said in the Townsend-moderated Curry vs. Al-Hajj debate that oil had already overshot to the downside — even before the additional $5 decline since that statement, suggesting even the bears see a near-term technical bounce.
insights.speaker: Anas Al-Hajj
insights.so_what: When the most vocal oil bear turns tactically bullish, it is a meaningful signal that the downside move is exhausted in the short run.

insights.claim: Ships didn't transit the Strait during the planting season, disrupting not just fuel but fertilizers, agricultural chemicals, and crop support inputs — Johnson expects a delayed 'food cascade' in Q4 2026 and Q1 2027 that the market is not pricing today.
insights.speaker: Brent Johnson
insights.so_what: Go long agricultural commodities with a Q4 2026/Q1 2027 time horizon; DBA Jan 2027 $27/$30 bull call spread (90-cent debit, max $2.10 payoff) is one expressed trade from the episode.

insights.claim: Patrick Szna's Trade of the Week: buy DBA (Invesco DB Agriculture ETF, trading at $26.84) January 15, 2027 $27/$30 bull call spread for a 90-cent net debit, with max payoff of $2.10 if DBA is at or above $30 by expiration — a better-than-2:1 risk/reward play on delayed agricultural tightening.
insights.speaker: Patrick Szna
insights.so_what: Specific actionable structure with defined risk and convexity to express the thesis that food supply shocks materialize 6-9 months after the shipping disruption.
insights.quote: The trade is to buy the $27 strike call option for $1.70 and sell the $30 strike call for 80, which creates a 27 by 30 bull call spread for a 90 net debit.

insights.claim: The Gulf region is a critical hub for global aviation MRO (Maintenance, Repair & Overhaul) operations; the Strait closure has disrupted this supply chain in a way that creates cascading disruptions into adjacent industries — a second-order effect Johnson says nobody else is discussing.
insights.speaker: Brent Johnson
insights.so_what: Watch for aviation supply-chain stress and delays in maintenance cycles; could benefit alternative MRO hubs or create cost pressure for airlines already stretched by fuel costs.

insights.claim: Supply chain nationalism has permanently broken the 'law of one price' for commodities: a given commodity now trades at materially different prices depending on where it is consumed, driven by tariffs, national security designations, and supply chain fragmentation — a regime change from the prior 25 years.
insights.speaker: Brent Johnson
insights.so_what: Commodity investors can no longer rely solely on a single global price; the spread between geographically-segmented markets is itself a tradeable variable.
insights.quote: Commodity prices over the last year have started to diverge based on where they're traded or where they're used. You can now have a different price for a commodity in the United States than you do in China, you do in Africa.

insights.claim: De-dollarization is largely a myth when measured by actual usage metrics: FX turnover, cross-border lending, and trade invoicing are all at all-time highs — the dollar's share of official reserves has fallen mainly relative to gold, not versus other fiat currencies.
insights.speaker: Brent Johnson
insights.so_what: The consensus 'dollar decline' narrative is unsupported by the data that actually measures dollar usage; dollar bear trades based on reserve share statistics are using the wrong metric.
insights.quote: If you look at things like FX turnover, if you look at crossborder lending, if you look at trade invoicing, they're all as high now as they have ever been and they're higher than they were 30 years ago.

insights.claim: Foreign holdings of US financial assets have nearly doubled since 2018 (when dollar-death predictions peaked), directly contradicting the de-dollarization narrative and validating the 'milkshake' thesis that capital would continue flowing into the US.
insights.speaker: Brent Johnson
insights.so_what: The milkshake thesis has a strong 8-year track record; continued dollar demand driven by foreign capital inflows supports US equity and credit markets structurally.

insights.claim: The 'dual carry trade' structural constraint means non-US sovereigns always have at least one carry trade working against them (local vs. dollar), while the US only faces its own currency carry dynamics — this is why dollar crises in EM are repetitive and dollar dominance is self-reinforcing.
insights.speaker: Brent Johnson
insights.so_what: The structural disadvantage of EM borrowers is permanent, not cyclical; dollar strength episodes will always cause disproportionate pain in EM — a recurring short opportunity in EM FX/credit.
insights.quote: The rest of the world because they have both a local carry trade in their local currency and they have a global carry trade in the dollar, they always have one of these carry trades is going against them.

insights.claim: Johnson's historical back-test: across 40 years and 16 EM case studies, 75% of the time when the dollar index moved outside its optimal 'band,' EM sovereigns went into distress and a dollar-funding crisis followed within a short time.
insights.speaker: Brent Johnson
insights.so_what: Watch the DXY band as a leading indicator for EM sovereign stress; a break above the band is a systematic signal to reduce EM exposure.

insights.claim: The US deliberately engineered a dollar shortage in Iran using Treasury tools, causing Iran's currency to collapse, driving domestic inflation, sparking street protests, and crushing the regime's revenue — demonstrating that 'weaponized dollar' operations are already in active use.
insights.speaker: Brent Johnson
insights.so_what: Countries subject to US financial pressure face weaponized dollar dynamics beyond just sanctions; the dollar system gives the US an asymmetric tool that can destabilize adversary economies without military action.
insights.quote: Bessant, Secretary Bessant just talked about this a few months ago where he said very specifically they used their tools to create a dollar shortage in Iran which caused their currency to fall very dramatically.

insights.claim: Gold fell ~20% from the Iran war outbreak to the peace deal announcement because nations (Russia, Turkey, Gulf states) that had been buying gold as an alternative reserve asset were forced to sell it to get dollars needed for oil purchases on global markets, illustrating that gold functions as a dollar-liquidity substitute that gets liquidated in crises.
insights.speaker: Brent Johnson
insights.so_what: Central bank gold reserves are not a true de-dollarization tool — they are liquidated precisely when dollars are scarce; the gold-as-reserve thesis has a critical Achilles heel.
insights.quote: From the moment the war broke out in Iran to the moment that it looked like we were getting this deal signed last week, gold fell 20%. The reason it fell 20% is those countries who had gold... started selling it... because they needed more dollars to buy oil on the global stage.

insights.claim: Rising US interest rates effectively imposed an 'early withdrawal penalty' on foreign dollar reserves held in Treasuries: when rates rose in 2022, bond prices fell and nations that needed to sell reserves to access dollars realized large losses — a covert wealth transfer to the US via the dollar system.
insights.speaker: Brent Johnson
insights.so_what: The US can simultaneously fight domestic inflation AND penalize foreign reserve holders; this dynamic will recur if/when the Fed tightens again.
insights.quote: That's essentially the same thing as an early withdrawal penalty being put on their dollar reserves.

themes.theme: Stablecoins as a Dollar Dominance Amplifier

insights.claim: 99% of all outstanding stablecoins globally are US dollar-denominated, not because it was mandated but because market demand is overwhelmingly for dollar-based digital money — a revealed preference that strongly argues against any BRICS or global-south stablecoin displacing the dollar.
insights.speaker: Brent Johnson
insights.so_what: Dollar stablecoins are a structural extension of dollar hegemony into digital rails; bet on continued dollar stablecoin dominance and be skeptical of BRICS coin narratives.
insights.quote: 99% of all outstanding stable coins in the world today are US dollar stable coins... That was the issuers responding to demand in the market.

insights.claim: As citizens in non-US countries choose to hold dollar stablecoins rather than their local currency, monetary sovereignty de facto transfers from local governments to Washington DC — Johnson describes this as 'an extremely powerful tool' that Treasury Secretary Bessant (a former currency speculator) is well-positioned to exploit.
insights.speaker: Brent Johnson
insights.so_what: Dollar stablecoin adoption in EM is a mechanism for gradual dollarization that bypasses capital controls; the US gains monetary leverage without firing a shot.
insights.quote: The transfer of sovereignty, monetary sovereignty starts to leave that local jurisdiction and that local government and get transferred to Washington DC.

insights.claim: Iran's oil — previously sold to China at a 20-30% discount in yuan on black markets — will now be sold at full price in dollars on open markets if the MOU holds, cutting off China's discounted yuan-denominated oil supply and forcing China to compete on dollar-priced markets.
insights.speaker: Brent Johnson
insights.so_what: If the MOU holds, China loses a large supply of discounted yuan-settled oil, which increases its dollar demand and weakens a key pillar of the yuan internationalization project.
insights.quote: Iran is now going to be able to sell that oil, at least for the time being, for full price and in dollars... China was paying anywhere from a 20 to 30% discount and using guess what not dollars but yuan.

themes.theme: Geopolitical Framework: US Hegemony, Deglobalization & Fourth Turning

insights.claim: Johnson's core macro framework: the world is transitioning from globalization to deglobalization and from a US-led rules-based multilateral order to American bilateral 'America First' deal-making — he expects this arc to persist regardless of which US party wins future elections.
insights.speaker: Brent Johnson
insights.so_what: Deglobalization is a durable regime, not a Trump-specific aberration; multi-year implications for supply chains, commodity regionalization, and EM vs. DM relative performance.
insights.quote: I have a hard time seeing even if the Democrats come back to power, I have a hard time seeing us going back to the rules-based order.

insights.claim: Johnson's most contrarian macro call: the US is more likely to transition from a republic to an empire than to yield hegemonic leadership to another power — he uses the historical analogy of Rome's transition from republic to empire (130-60 BC) as the most applicable historical parallel to today.
insights.speaker: Brent Johnson
insights.so_what: If the US-as-empire thesis is correct, institutions that constrain executive power will weaken, military spending will remain structurally high, and 'USD dies' scenarios are extremely premature.
insights.quote: I think it's more likely we see the fall of the republic and the rise of the empire than the fall of the empire and some new power takeover.

insights.claim: The Iran conflict revealed that Russia and China will not come to the direct military rescue of a key ally (Iran) despite geopolitical alignment — a significant intelligence finding with implications for the credibility of adversary security guarantees in future conflicts.
insights.speaker: Brent Johnson
insights.so_what: The BRICS security umbrella is weaker than advertised; countries betting on Russian or Chinese protection in a crisis with the US should not trust it will materialize.
insights.quote: We also found out that Russia and China will not come into the rescue of one of their biggest allies despite the fact that they need Iran.

insights.claim: The Iran conflict achieved several US strategic intelligence objectives beyond the stated nuclear goal: confirmed Iran's military capabilities (including a missile that can reach Europe), verified Gulf states deepened ties with the US rather than pivoting away, and exposed OPEC's diminishing relevance.
insights.speaker: Brent Johnson
insights.so_what: Even an 'incomplete' military campaign can yield strategic information that reshapes alliance calculus and future deterrence; OPEC's declining relevance is now an empirical fact, not just a forecast.

insights.claim: Closing the Strait benefited both the US and Iran, but the US used the closure as economic leverage over third-party nations to extract better terms in bilateral trade and energy deals: since Feb 26, multiple large long-term energy contracts have been signed, including a new deal with Japan signed ~4-5 days before the episode.
insights.speaker: Brent Johnson
insights.so_what: Countries that signed favorable energy agreements with the US during the crisis are now locked into long-term supply relationships that reinforce dollar-denominated energy trade.
insights.quote: The US is not only negotiating with Iran, they are negotiating with the rest of the world. And when the rest of the world is under pressure, the US can exact better terms on whatever deals that they're negotiating.

insights.claim: Johnson distinguishes two distinct US global strategies: the 'greater good / multilateral' approach (Keynesian, post-WWII, UN/WTO/World Bank) versus the 'America First / bilateral' approach — and argues both have the same end goal (US on top) but completely different execution and side-effect profiles for the rest of the world.
insights.speaker: Brent Johnson
insights.so_what: Understanding which strategic mode the US is in determines how to think about dollar flows, alliance formation, and EM vulnerability; the current mode favors dollar strength and bilateral energy deals.

insights.claim: National defense is Johnson's highest-conviction investment theme: every country will need to rearm, and money will be spent on defense regardless of fiscal constraints — he has launched a private fund specifically targeting the US Office of Strategic Capital to invest in this sector.
insights.speaker: Brent Johnson
insights.so_what: Global defense spending is a structural multi-year theme; defense contractors in both the US and allied nations should see sustained demand regardless of the economic cycle.
insights.quote: National defense is I think probably going to be the biggest investment opportunity any of us have ever seen or one of the biggest opportunities any of us have ever seen both in the United States and abroad.

insights.claim: SpaceX IPO lock-up structure: if SpaceX trades above the IPO price for 5 of 10 days during a window starting in early July, 10% of insider lock-up shares unlock immediately — Townsend flags this as a potential near-term overhang risk alongside OpenAI and Anthropic IPOs absorbing massive capital.
insights.speaker: Erik Townsend
insights.so_what: Three mega-IPOs (SpaceX, OpenAI, Anthropic) plus early lock-up expirations could create significant secondary supply pressure on risk assets as early as mid-July.

insights.claim: Townsend expects a sector rotation out of the last 6 months' winners (energy stocks and semiconductors) into laggards (financials, defense contractors, industrials) as the oil crisis narrative recedes and market breadth widens.
insights.speaker: Patrick Szna
insights.so_what: A broadening of market breadth would reward equal-weight or value tilts over growth/energy concentration; specific beneficiaries include financials and defense.

insights.claim: Kevin Worsh's first FOMC dot plot as Fed Chair already signals a hike is more likely than a cut as the first policy action — market pricing for rapid cuts under Worsh was wrong; Townsend says gold could face sharp pressure at the September FOMC if the Fed hikes.
insights.speaker: Erik Townsend
insights.so_what: The 'new Fed chair will cut immediately' trade is invalidated; repricing of rate-cut expectations is a headwind for gold in the near term and supportive of the dollar.

insights.claim: Uranium remains strategically bullish long-term (driven by AI data center power demand) but is in a seasonal soft patch; Townsend expects the market to re-engage around the World Nuclear Association conference in London in early September.
insights.speaker: Erik Townsend
insights.so_what: Late August/early September is the seasonal entry point for uranium equities; the sector's current chart distribution could convert to accumulation near the WNA conference catalyst.

insights.claim: The USD/JPY rate is quietly breaking above 160 — a level that previously triggered multiple Bank of Japan interventions — despite the BOJ raising interest rates with almost no market reaction; this implies a whole new leg down for the yen may be beginning.
insights.speaker: Patrick Szna
insights.so_what: USD/JPY breaking decisively above 160 with no BOJ intervention effectiveness is a structural yen weakness signal; long USD/JPY or short JPY-funded carry-trade reversals.
insights.quote: The 160 level on the USNY has been a rock solid overhead resistance where we have seen numerous interventions happen in the past and yet here we are quietly crawling above that level.

insights.claim: Gold's near-term outlook: the 4.5% bounce to ~$4,321 on the Iran deal has not produced a bullish trend reversal; Townsend is watching for gold to stop making lower lows and for structural accumulation signals, but won't rule out a retest of prior lows; a genuine bull market in gold likely awaits a pivot in rates and inflation expectations.
insights.speaker: Patrick Szna
insights.so_what: Do not chase the gold bounce; wait for structural accumulation evidence and a Fed pivot signal before sizing into a new gold bull market position.
